ac:rich-text-body.p: Detected Filterbank data is a time-frequency Observatory Data Product used for discovering and characterising pulsars and transient events like fast radio bursts.

p: Detected Filterbank (DFB) data is a specialised Observatory Data Product designed for discovering and characterising transient and periodic phenomena in radio observations. It consists of time-frequency intensity measurements optimised for pulsar and fast radio burst (FRB) searches.
Detected Filterbank data has the following structure:

ul.li: Time axis: Sequential samples from the observation, typically at millisecond or sub-millisecond resolution
Frequency axis: Filterbank channels spanning the observation bandwidth, typically 10–100 MHz resolution per channel
Polarisation: Linear (XX, XY, YX, YY) or Stokes parameters (I, Q, U, V)
Intensity: Real-valued power or amplitude, after RF band-limiting and detection

p: DFB data is produced by the SKA signal processing pipeline and archived in standard formats including:

ul.li: FITS-format filterbank files compatible with standard radio astronomy tools
HDF5 binary archives for efficient storage and access
Metadata headers documenting observation parameters, calibration, and processing

p: Detected Filterbank data is generated by the SKA signal processing pipeline via the following steps:

ol.li: RF Band-Limiting: Wide-bandwidth antenna signals are filtered to the observation band
Channelisation: The band is divided into narrow frequency channels by a polyphase filterbank or FFT
Detection: Power or intensity is computed for each time-frequency bin (often via squared magnitude of complex visibilities or beamformed outputs)
Integration: Time-frequency samples may be integrated over short intervals to control data volume
Formatting: Data are packaged into standard archive formats with full metadata

ul.li: Flux density calibration relative to known radio sources
Bandpass calibration to correct for frequency-dependent gain variations
Gain and phase correction metadata
RFI flagging information indicating contaminated samples

p: DFB data enables efficient pulsar searches via dedispersion and folding algorithms. Key applications include:

ul.li: Discovery of new millisecond pulsars and ultra-long-period objects
Pulsar timing measurements for gravitational wave detection via pulsar timing arrays
Characterisation of pulsar emission mechanisms and magnetosphere physics

p: The time-frequency structure of DFB data is ideal for identifying impulsive transients:

ul.li: FRB discovery and localisation through multi-beam transient identification
Measurement of dispersion measure and rotation measure
Statistical studies of the FRB population

p: Beyond pulsars and FRBs, DFB data can be used to search for:

ul.li: Rotating transients and rotating radio transients (RRATs)
Sporadic burst sources and orphan afterglows
Magnetars and other exotic neutron star phenomena

p: DFB data rates depend on observation configuration:

ul.li: SKA-Low observations: Approximately 100 GB to 1 TB per observation hour (frequency-dependent)
SKA-Mid observations: Similar ranges with different frequency coverage and resolution trade-offs

p: These rates reflect a balance between scientific sensitivity (fine time-frequency resolution) and archive storage constraints. Integration and selective archival strategies are employed to manage volume.
All DFB data are archived in the SKA Archive with:

ul.li: Comprehensive metadata indexing for discovery
Long-term preservation guarantees
On-demand access and download capabilities

p: Users can search for and download DFB data via the SKA Archive portal using filters such as:

ul.li: Observation date and time
Sky region (RA/Dec or object name)
Frequency range
Observation type (pulsar, transient, blind survey)
Quality metrics and RFI levels

p: Standard tools for working with DFB data include:

ul.li: PRESTO: Pulsar searching and analysis
SIGPROC: Filterbank file manipulation and processing
Your: Your Unified Reduction code for modern Python-based analysis

p: The SKA provides containerised versions of these tools and example Jupyter notebooks to help new users get started.
For large DFB datasets, users can run analysis directly on archive-hosted data via SKA computational services, avoiding the need to download multi-terabyte files locally.

ac:rich-text-body.p: See also Pulsar Timing Observatory Data Product for high-precision timing measurements and Introduction to Observatory Data Products and Advanced Data Products for the full ODP suite.
